name: Application of Integrals
subject: Maths

sections.heading: 8.1 Area Under a Simple Curve
sections.body:
The area bounded by the curve $y=f(x)\geq 0$, the x-axis and the vertical lines $x=a$, $x=b$ is

$A=\int_{a}^{b} y\,dx=\int_{a}^{b} f(x)\,dx.$

If the curve lies below the x-axis ($f(x)<0$) on $[a,b]$, the integral is negative; the geometric area is its absolute value: $A=\left|\,\int_a^b f(x)\,dx\,\right|$.

If $f$ changes sign in $[a,b]$, split the integral at the zeros and add the absolute values: $A=\int_a^c|f|\,dx+\int_c^b|f|\,dx$.
keyFormulae: Split at zeros if $f$ changes sign

sections.heading: 8.2 Area Bounded by $x=g(y)$
sections.body:
If a curve is more naturally written $x=g(y)$ and the area is bounded between $y=c$ and $y=d$ and the y-axis, then

$A=\int_{c}^{d} x\,dy=\int_{c}^{d} g(y)\,dy.$

This is the right approach when the curve has horizontal tangents or wraps onto itself in $x$.

sections.heading: 8.3 Area Between Two Curves
sections.body:
If $f(x)\geq g(x)$ on $[a,b]$, the area between $y=f(x)$ (upper) and $y=g(x)$ (lower) over this interval is

$A=\int_{a}^{b}\big[f(x)-g(x)\big]\,dx.$

Procedure.

  1. Find points of intersection by solving $f(x)=g(x)$.
  2. Determine which curve is on top in each sub-interval (sketch or test a value).
  3. Integrate (upper $-$ lower).
  4. If the upper/lower roles swap, split and sum the appropriate absolute differences.

sections.heading: 8.4 Standard Geometric Examples
sections.body:
Circle. Area of $x^{2}+y^{2}=a^{2}$ is $4\!\int_0^{a}\!\sqrt{a^{2}-x^{2}}\,dx=\pi a^{2}.$

Ellipse. $\dfrac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\dfrac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$ has area $4\!\int_0^{a}\dfrac{b}{a}\sqrt{a^{2}-x^{2}}\,dx=\pi ab.$

Parabola. Area enclosed by $y^{2}=4ax$ and the line $x=h$ is $\dfrac{8}{3}a^{1/2}h^{3/2}$.

sections.heading: 8.5 Worked Approach for Mixed Curves
sections.body:
For a region enclosed by, say, a parabola and a line:

  1. Solve simultaneously to find intersection points.
  2. Sketch to identify the bounded region.
  3. Decide whether to integrate w.r.t. $x$ or $y$ — pick the variable that avoids splitting the integral.
  4. Set up $\int(\text{upper}-\text{lower})$ between intersection abscissae (or ordinates).
  5. Evaluate using standard techniques from Ch 7.

Example. Region bounded by $y^{2}=x$ and $y=x$ in the first quadrant: intersections at $(0,0)$ and $(1,1)$; area $=\int_0^{1}(\sqrt{x}-x)\,dx=\dfrac{1}{6}$.
keyFormulae: Choose integration variable to avoid splitting

summary: Definite integrals compute geometric areas. For $y=f(x)$ above the x-axis between $x=a$ and $x=b$ the area is $\int_a^b f(x)dx$; below the axis use absolute value. Curves expressed as $x=g(y)$ are integrated w.r.t. $y$. The area enclosed between two curves is $\int(\text{upper}-\text{lower})$. Classical results include circle $\pi a^{2}$ and ellipse $\pi ab$. Always sketch the region, find intersection points, and split the integral wherever the upper/lower roles swap.
pitfalls: Forgetting the sign of $f$ — area is the absolute value of the integral when $f<0$.
Not splitting at points where the upper/lower curves swap.
Failing to find ALL intersection points before setting up integration limits.
Mixing up which variable to integrate w.r.t. — pick the one that lets you write a single integral.
Confusing area with arc length — only one is on the syllabus.
